title: Pronouncing Endings: -er, -est, -ly
learningGoal: Master the pronunciation of comparative (-er), superlative (-est), and adverbial (-ly) endings, as well as irregular forms like better/best.
instructions: 比較級（-er）、最上級（-est）、副詞（-ly）の語尾を正しく発音する練習です。また、better や worst のような不規則な単語も練習します。マイクに向かってリピートしてください。(Practice pronouncing the endings for comparatives, superlatives, and adverbs. Repeat into the microphone.)

targetPhrasesOrWords.phoneticHint: The '-er' sound is soft. Pull your tongue back slightly for the 'r'.
Don't pause between quiet and ly. Flow them together: qui-et-ly.
The 't' often sounds like a soft 'd' or flap 'r' in American English. Beau-di-ful-ly.
In American English, the 'tt' sounds like a quick, soft 'd' (flap T). Be-der.
Make sure to say 'the'. The 't' in best is often very soft.
Make your lips round for the 'w', and pull your tongue back for the 'r'. Werr-ss.
Add a crisp 't' at the end of worse. Werr-st.
Make sure to pronounce the 'r' at the end. Morrr.
The 'o' is a long 'oh' sound. Moh-st.

expectedPronunciationText: taller tallest quietly beautifully better the best worse the worst more the most

feedbackRules.onPass: Excellent! You pronounced the word endings and the irregular words very clearly.
feedbackRules.onFail: Please try again. Pay close attention to the 'r' sounds at the ends of words like taller and better.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Remember that 'est' usually sounds like 'ist' (tall-ist).

adminCorrectAnswerSet.exactCorrectTranscript: taller tallest quietly beautifully better the best worse the worst more the most
acceptedVariants: taller tallest quietly beautifully better the best worse the worst more the most
adminCorrectAnswerSet.nativeScriptReference: taller tallest quietly beautifully better the best worse the worst more the most
adminCorrectAnswerSet.transliteration: tawler tawlist kwietly byootifuly beder the best wers the werst mor the mohst

audioAssets.audioTranscript: taller, tallest, quietly, beautifully, better, the best, worse, the worst, more, the most

hintText: In American English, 'better' sounds like 'bedder'. Give it a try!
